title: Traumatic Brain Injury
description: Traumatic brain injuries can result from car accidents, falls, workplace incidents, or any event involving a blow to the head. TBI symptoms can be delayed, misdiagnosed, or worsen over time — making early medical evaluation and thorough documentation essential to protecting your health and your claim.
metaTitle: Traumatic Brain Injury Guide — Steps, Evidence & Legal Options
metaDescription: Suffered a traumatic brain injury in an accident? Get guidance on immediate steps, evidence to collect, insurance issues, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Motor Vehicle Accidents
commonCauses.description: Car, truck, and motorcycle crashes are the leading cause of TBI — the sudden force of impact causes the brain to move inside the skull.

commonCauses.title: Slip and Fall Accidents
commonCauses.description: Falls are the second leading cause of TBI, especially on hard surfaces or from elevated heights.

commonCauses.title: Workplace Accidents
commonCauses.description: Construction sites, warehouses, and industrial settings carry high risk of head trauma from falling objects or equipment strikes.

commonCauses.title: Struck by an Object
commonCauses.description: Being hit by a falling, thrown, or moving object — common in construction and sports — can cause significant brain trauma.

commonCauses.title: Assault or Physical Altercation
commonCauses.description: Intentional blows to the head can result in TBI and may support both criminal and civil claims.

commonCauses.title: Sports and Recreational Activities
commonCauses.description: Contact sports, cycling without a helmet, and high-impact activities carry TBI risk, though these cases differ in legal liability.

immediateSteps.title: Call 911 or go to the emergency room immediately
immediateSteps.description: Any suspected head injury should be evaluated by emergency medical professionals. Do not wait for symptoms to worsen — some TBIs are not obvious at the scene.

immediateSteps.title: Do not leave the scene without medical documentation
immediateSteps.description: If paramedics respond, let them evaluate you. Their report becomes part of your medical record and links the incident to the injury.

immediateSteps.title: Document the incident scene
immediateSteps.description: If safe, photograph the location, any hazards, vehicles involved, and anything that caused or contributed to the head impact.

immediateSteps.title: Start a symptom journal immediately
immediateSteps.description: TBI symptoms — headaches, memory gaps, confusion, mood changes, sleep disruption — may appear or worsen over days. Log every symptom with dates and severity.

immediateSteps.title: Follow up with a neurologist or specialist
immediateSteps.description: An ER visit may not capture the full extent of a TBI. Request a referral for neurological evaluation, MRI, or cognitive testing.

immediateSteps.title: Notify your insurance company
immediateSteps.description: Report the incident to your insurer promptly. Stick to facts — do not minimize symptoms or speculate about severity.

immediateSteps.title: Avoid giving recorded statements without legal guidance
immediateSteps.description: TBI claims often involve disputed causation. Before speaking with any opposing insurer, consider consulting with a personal injury attorney.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve evidence of lost function
immediateSteps.description: Document missed work, tasks you can no longer perform, and any assistance you require. This supports future wage loss and quality-of-life claims.

items: Emergency room or urgent care records from date of incident
All neurologist and specialist evaluation notes
MRI, CT scan, and EEG reports
Neuropsychological testing results
Physical therapy and occupational therapy records
Prescription records for TBI-related medications
Ongoing symptom journal (dates, symptoms, severity)

evidenceChecklist.category: At the Scene or Incident Location
items: Photos of the location where the head injury occurred
Photos of any object, vehicle, or hazard that caused the impact
Police or incident report number
Names and contact information of witnesses
Surveillance or dashcam footage if available
Employer documentation of missed work days
Pay stubs and earnings records to calculate lost wages
Records of tasks you can no longer perform
Statements from family members about changes in behavior or function
Documentation of any in-home care or assistance required
Your health insurance and auto insurance policy documents
All correspondence with insurance adjusters
Any settlement offers received in writing
Copy of any police, workplace, or incident report
Records of ongoing cognitive or memory difficulties
Documented impact on personal relationships
Future medical cost estimates from treating physicians
Life care plan if long-term care is anticipated

timelineRisks.risk: Delayed TBI symptoms may go undiagnosed
timelineRisks.action: Seek neurological evaluation even if initial ER findings appear mild. Concussions and diffuse axonal injuries are often underdetected in early imaging.

timelineRisks.risk: Cognitive symptoms may be attributed to stress rather than injury
timelineRisks.action: Begin your symptom journal. Follow up with a specialist and request neuropsychological testing if symptoms persist.
Notify all relevant insurers promptly. Delays can create coverage disputes.

timelineRisks.risk: Witness memories fade, scene evidence is gone
timelineRisks.action: Secure statements from witnesses and collect any available surveillance footage before it is overwritten or deleted.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: California generally requires a personal injury lawsuit to be filed within 2 years of the injury date. TBI cases involving government entities may have much shorter deadlines.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Arizona has a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims. Do not wait — TBI cases require extensive preparation and medical documentation.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Insurers frequently argue that a TBI was pre-existing, caused by a different event, or is less severe than claimed. Thorough and timely medical documentation is essential to counter these arguments.
TBI cases often have long-term consequences that are not apparent in the early weeks. Accepting a settlement before you understand the full extent of your injury can permanently close your claim.
Insurers may require you to be evaluated by a physician of their choosing. These IME doctors are paid by the insurer and their findings often favor a quick resolution.

insuranceIssues.issue: Wage loss and future earnings disputes
insuranceIssues.explanation: Insurers may challenge the amount of income lost due to your injury. Vocational experts and life care planners are often needed to document long-term economic impact.
TBI cases often involve significant pain, suffering, and loss of enjoyment of life. These non-economic damages can be substantial but are subject to negotiation and — in some contexts — legal caps.

whenToGetLawyer: You were diagnosed with any grade of concussion, brain bleed, or traumatic brain injury
Symptoms persisted beyond 2 weeks or worsened over time
You missed significant work or are unable to return to your prior occupation
An insurer is disputing whether the injury was caused by the accident
You received an early settlement offer before completing your medical treatment
You are being required to attend an insurer-arranged independent medical examination
Your injury has affected your cognitive function, memory, personality, or relationships
The incident involved a commercial vehicle, employer, or property owner
